perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall be responsible for the management and control over the services provided by its staff members operating the Stands ("Sponsor Staff") and Sponsor shall be solely responsible for determining the terms of employment for Sponsor Staff. Sponsor shall train Sponsor Staff or require Sponsor Staff to undergo training provided by Concessionaire. The staffing levels at each Stand shall be subject to Club's approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor shall be responsible for the management and control over the services provided by its staff members operating the Stands ("Sponsor Staff"). The staffing levels at each Stand shall be subject to Club's approval.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit responsibilities of the Sponsor to determine terms of employment and provide training for its staff, the agreement becomes vague, potentially opening the door for violations of minimum wage, worker's compensation, and anti-discrimination laws. In Florida, employers have specific legal obligations to their employees, which this omission obscures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) and Florida Minimum Wage Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the FLSA (29 U.S. Code § 206) and Florida Statutes § 448.110 because it removes the sponsor's explicit responsibility for determining terms of employment and training staff, which implicitly removes the adherence to minimum wage and labor laws required for employees.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states the Sponsor's responsibilities regarding employment terms and staff training. The changed text omits these responsibilities, which, according to the Perturbation and Law Explanations, could lead to non-compliance with labor laws such as minimum wage and anti-discrimination laws. The absence of explicit duties related to employment terms in the changed text, as compared to the original, creates a loophole where the Sponsor's obligations to adhere to labor laws are not clearly defined, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: b. Vending: Subject to the Concessions Agreement (as defined herein), Sponsor shall have the right to have its food products sold or otherwise distributed from the Stands and/or certain general concessions areas at the Stadium determined by Club or the Concessionaire. For purposes of clarity, nothing in this Agreement grants Sponsor the right to operate the Stand or otherwise sell or distribute food products from or within the Stand. Sponsor shall enter into an agreement with the Concessionaire to memorialize any such rights regarding the sale or distribution of Sponsor's products at the Stadium during each Jaguars Home Game and Other Events (the "Concessions Agreement"). The exact products to be sold and distributed shall be subject to Club's final approval. For purposes of clarity, Club reserves the right to sell other products at the Stadium competitive to the Sponsor Business. Sponsor acknowledges that such rights do not automatically extend to Other Events at the Stadium. Sponsor acknowledges that Sponsor shall cooperate with the Concessionaire regarding logistics and management of the Sponsor's food products, and appropriate storage and dispensation of the food products. In the event of any recall with respect to Sponsor's products provided to the Concessionaire pursuant to this Agreement or the Concessions Agreement, Sponsor shall notify both Club and the Concessionaire immediately upon issuance of such recall, and Club may, at its sole discretion and without penalty, suspend the Benefits for a duration as reasonably determined by Club. Any costs or expenses incurred by Club or the Concessionaire with respect to any such recall shall be the sole responsibility of Sponsor.
perturbation.changed_text: b. Vending: Subject to the Concessions Agreement (as defined herein), Sponsor shall have the right to have its food products sold or otherwise distributed from the Stands and/or certain general concessions areas at the Stadium determined by Club or the Concessionaire. For purposes of clarity, nothing in this Agreement grants Sponsor the right to operate the Stand or otherwise sell or distribute food products from or within the Stand. Sponsor shall enter into an agreement with the Concessionaire to memorialize any such rights regarding the sale or distribution of Sponsor's products at the Stadium during each Jaguars Home Game and Other Events (the "Concessions Agreement"). The exact products to be sold and distributed shall be subject to Club's final approval. For purposes of clarity, Club reserves the right to sell other products at the Stadium competitive to the Sponsor Business. Sponsor acknowledges that such rights do not automatically extend to Other Events at the Stadium. Sponsor acknowledges that Sponsor shall cooperate with the Concessionaire regarding logistics and management of the Sponsor's food products, and appropriate storage and dispensation of the food products.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the recall notification clause introduces legal risk. Food recalls are governed by strict regulations, and failure to notify relevant parties promptly can lead to legal penalties. This creates a loophole where the sponsor might not be held accountable for recall-related responsibilities, potentially violating consumer safety laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the recall notification requirement, the modified text contradicts 21 U.S.C. § 350l, which outlines the FDA's authority and procedures for food recalls. While the law doesn't explicitly mandate the contracted party to notify the other, it implicitly sets the basis of consumer protection against unsafe food, making any contract that prevents notification non-compliant with the spirit and intent of the law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text included a clause requiring the Sponsor to notify both the Club and the Concessionaire immediately upon issuance of a recall. The modified text omits this clause, removing the sponsor's obligation to notify these parties. The perturbation explanation highlights that this creates a loophole where the sponsor might not be held accountable for recall-related responsibilities, potentially violating consumer safety laws. The law explanation states that removing the recall notification requirement contradicts 21 U.S.C. § 350l, setting a basis of consumer protection against unsafe food. Although scraped snippet 1 is missing and snippet 2 is empty, the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation and the omission of a legally mandated consumer protection clause in the changed text, clearly establishes a contradiction because it removes a requirement related to food safety and recall procedures.

perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall deliver the food (the cost of which is included in the Annual Trade Value) to the Stadium at the time and location specified by Club. If any portion of the Annual Trade Value is not used in any given Contract Year, such unused amount shall carry forward to the subsequent Contract Year. If any portion of the Annual Trade Value is not used at the end of the Term, Club shall be permitted to use such unused amount within twelve (12) months following expiration of this Agreement. The parties acknowledge that the Annual Trade Value is inclusive of any taxes, surcharges or related fees applicable to the orders placed by Club during the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor shall deliver the food (the cost of which is included in the Annual Trade Value) to the Stadium at the time and location specified by Club. If any portion of the Annual Trade Value is not used at the end of the Term, Club shall be permitted to use such unused amount within twelve (12) months following expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that unused amounts carry forward to the next contract year creates a situation where the Club may lose out on value without recourse if trade values are not fulfilled during a contract year. By stating it must be used in the next twelve months, it implies that the company no longer has a responsibility to provide the benefits within the contractually obligated seasons. This can contradict Florida's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute on Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Florida law recognizes an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. (No specific statute, but implied from case law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the clause about carry-over of unused amounts contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in Florida contract law. While not explicitly codified in a single statute, Florida courts recognize this covenant. By removing the carry-over provision, the Club loses value in a year where the full value was not received; thus the contract's benefits may be unfairly diminished, and this clause creates a potential basis for a claim of bad faith dealing.

perturbation.justification: The changed text omits a clause that allows unused amounts to carry over to the subsequent contract year, potentially diminishing the Club's benefits and creating a basis for a bad faith dealing claim, as the Club loses value in a year where the full value was not received. The Perturbation Explanation supports this assertion, stating that the removal creates a situation where the Club may lose out on value without recourse. The Law Explanation supports this further, connecting the change to a potential violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in Florida contract law. Although the scraped snippets do not mention the implied covenant of good faith, the argument from the law and perturbation explanations is strong.
